narrative: I'm sorry to share that your law school application was rejected because our predictive model estimates a 59.9% chance you'll fail the bar exam on your first try, which is above the threshold we use for admissions decisions. Your profile includes being in the lowest decile for grades in both your first and third years of school—much lower than the typical student's mid-range performance—along with an LSAT score of 31, well below the average of about 37, and an undergraduate GPA of 3.10, slightly under the usual 3.21. You're from an upper-middle-income family, identify as Hispanic, are female, and plan to attend full-time, which aligns with most applicants. The model's decision was driven primarily by your low Year 3 school decile ranking, which had the strongest influence in pushing the prediction toward failure since it's far below what's common for successful bar takers. Next in importance was your similarly low Year 1 decile, which also significantly nudged the odds against passing. Your LSAT score ranked third in influence, again pulling strongly toward a fail prediction due to how much lower it is than average. Factors like your family income, racial background as Hispanic, and undergraduate GPA followed closely, each contributing further to the higher failure risk compared to typical students who tend to have stronger profiles in these areas. Your full-time enrollment status had minimal impact, while being female slightly helped by pushing the prediction a bit toward success, though not enough to offset the others. Overall, these academic performance indicators were the key drivers, far outweighing demographics or background. To improve your future chances, focus on what you can realistically change: retake the LSAT to aim closer to or above the average score, strengthen any upcoming grades or advanced coursework to boost your decile rankings, and seek targeted bar exam prep resources tailored for students with similar starting points. With dedicated effort in these areas, you could shift your predicted odds dramatically in a future application. Keep pushing forward—you have the determination to turn this around.
